title: 命案目睹记
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\136-命案目睹记-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a quiet breakfast, Miss Marple and Mrs. McGillicuddy walk in the garden, discussing their respective physical ailments. Miss Marple mentions restrictions imposed by Dr. Heelis, while Mrs. McGillicuddy notes her own discomfort when bending. Miss Marple then suggests they visit the police station to speak with Inspector Frank Cornish.

At the station, Mrs. McGillicuddy recounts her account of witnessing a potential murder. Inspector Cornish listens attentively and, while acknowledging Mrs. McGillicuddy is a sensible woman, suggests the incident might have been a misunderstanding or a prank. He informs her that she has fulfilled her duty by reporting the matter to both the railway authorities and the police. Miss Marple and Mrs. McGillicuddy subsequently discuss how a killer might dispose of a body, considering whether it was left on the train or thrown from the tracks, noting that a woman being helped off a train would likely have left a hospital record.

The following evening, Inspector Cornish sends a note stating that a thorough investigation found no female body, no matching hospital records, and no evidence of a man helping a woman at the station. He concludes that while Mrs. McGillicuddy likely witnessed something, it was not as severe as she believed. This news upsets Mrs. McGillicuddy, who insists it is a murder case. As Mrs. McGillicuddy considers whether she should cancel her upcoming trip to Ceylon to visit Rodrick, Miss Marple remains convinced that the killer is clever. Miss Marple speculates that the murder might have been a crime of passion involving strangulation, where the killer hid the body in a corner seat to make the victim appear asleep before fleeing the train.
